one_line_takeaway: AgentMark embeds multi-bit provenance IDs into an LLM agent's planning-level tool/subgoal choices via distribution-preserving sampling (rather than logit biasing), avoiding the significant utility loss (up to 10.7-point success-rate drop on ALFWorld) that a bias-based watermark baseline incurs, while achieving near-zero false positives at moderate overhead and RLNC-based recovery from partial logs -- though recovery degrades sharply (bit recovery falls to ~17%) under semantic-preserving rewriting of logged observations.
key_finding: AgentMark-F preserves task utility essentially at baseline levels while the RG bias-based baseline measurably degrades it: on ALFWorld-ID, RG drops average success rate from 89.5% to 78.8% (down 10.7 points) and increases average steps from 19.7 to 26.1 (up 6.4 steps), whereas AgentMark-F stays close to baseline (89.3% SR, down only 0.2 points; 19.4 steps, down 0.3). On ALFWorld-OOD, AgentMark-F actually slightly exceeds baseline SR (97.5% vs 96.8%) with fewer steps (14.1 vs 15.9), while RG again degrades (94.5% SR, 15.4 steps). On ToolBench, AgentMark-F averages 59.7% SR (vs 59.9% baseline, vs 58.5% for RG) with average token-per-step change of -6.25% (often lower due to earlier termination) and per-step latency overhead near zero (-1.27 ms average). Watermark capacity: ~1.19-1.34 bits/step on ALFWorld (1.19 ID, 1.34 OOD) and 0.49 bits/step / 4.93 bits/task on ToolBench. Robustness: false-positive rate for unwatermarked/wrong-key decoding drops below 1% at overhead k>=8 and reaches zero observed false positives at k>=14, tracking the theoretical 2^-k bound. RLNC coding is substantially more resilient to step erasure than a repetition-code baseline, with global (cross-trajectory) aggregation further improving recovery via a phase-transition effect once enough independent equations accumulate. Under semantic-preserving observation rewriting (the paper's own designated stress test), behavior match rate drops to 49.45% +/- 16.90%, average KL divergence between elicited distributions rises to 3.227 +/- 1.802, and bit recovery rate falls to 16.84% +/- 19.56% -- i.e., the mechanism's synchronization assumption is fragile under adversarial or lossy paraphrasing of logs, though the paper argues aggregation over many long-horizon steps can still support practical verification since payloads are short relative to trajectory length. In composability tests (Table 3), stacking AgentMark-F with SynthID-Text preserved 100% correct behavioral decoding for AgentMark-F and 96.6% SynthID-Text content-detection accuracy on ToolBench.
core_contribution: AgentMark is a behavioral watermarking framework for LLM-based agents that embeds multi-bit provenance identifiers into an agent's planning-level decisions (tool/subgoal choices) rather than its generated content. Instead of biasing the agent's decision probabilities (which the paper shows causes compounding distribution shift and utility loss over long horizons), AgentMark elicits an explicit behavior probability distribution P_t from the agent at each planning step and applies distribution-preserving conditional sampling to select a watermarked behavior whose marginal exactly matches the elicited distribution, so the induced behavior policy is provably unchanged. The paper instantiates this general paradigm as AgentMark-F, using an FDPSS-style construction (differential probability recombination into a mixture of uniform bins, keyed pseudorandom bin/index selection, and cyclic-shift uniform encoding within a bin) to embed variable-length bitstrings, plus a random-linear-network-coding (RLNC) payload layer over GF(2) for erasure-resilient recovery when planning-time logs are partially missing (step erasure) or truncated.
threat_model: Provenance verification, not adversarial robustness in the classic sense. Setting: a user gives an agent a high-level goal; the agent autonomously executes a multi-step plan with limited human oversight; the verifier (holding a shared secret key K_sh with the embedder) later inspects logged planning decisions to recover an embedded ID. The verifier may see only a subset I of the T total planning steps because of incomplete logging, execution failures, or platform-side deletion (parameterized by an erasure rate rho) or only a truncated prefix (trajectory truncation). Post-hoc edits to execution-action arguments are treated as effectively producing missing/shortened planning-time records rather than modeled as a distinct adversary. The paper also stress-tests (Sec 5.3) semantic-preserving rewriting of step observations as a threat to encoder/decoder synchronization, and separately evaluates false-positive/key-forgery rates for wrong-key or unwatermarked decoding attempts. This is a provenance/IP-protection and regulatory-auditing threat model (attributing agent behavior, detecting misuse/unauthorized replication), not a jailbreak, prompt-injection, or model-extraction threat model.
limitations: Limitations stated explicitly by the authors: (1) AgentMark requires the agent to output an explicit planning-time behavior probability distribution over a predefined behavior list and requires logging of planning-time decisions plus minimal step context for verification -- deployments unable to retain sufficient logs limit provenance recovery. (2) Verification relies on reproducing per-step randomness from logged observation context, so semantic variation in observations (e.g., paraphrasing) reduces synchronization and degrades verification quality -- demonstrated empirically by the sharp drop in bit recovery rate (16.84%) under semantic rewriting. (3) Because the scheme strictly preserves the original behavior distribution, per-step embedding capacity is inherently context-dependent and can be small when P_t is highly peaked (near-deterministic), limiting per-episode capacity without cross-step/cross-trajectory aggregation. (4) The approach depends on eliciting an explicit probability list from the agent (works for black-box APIs that can be prompted to output probabilities) rather than reading true model logits; for open-source/white-box LLMs, extracting the planning-time policy directly from logits or latent representations is left as future work. Additional caveats visible in the reviewed text: the RLNC false-positive/robustness experiments were built from a relatively narrow slice of ToolBench (281 trajectories, subsets T1-T3 only, 159 unique action indices), and the semantic-rewriting stress test covers only ALFWorld-OOD, so generalization of the rewriting-fragility result to ToolBench/OASIS is not directly measured in the excerpted sections. The threat model also does not consider an adaptive adversary who knows the watermarking scheme and deliberately manipulates the elicited probability distribution itself (only passing references to 'adaptive threat models' appear, deferred to an appendix not captured in this extraction).

datasets_used: ALFWorld (ID and OOD splits; embodied task benchmark)

ToolBench (six subsets T1-T6: single-tool selection, single-tool instruction-following, single-tool within-category selection, multi-tool within-category selection, multi-tool instruction-following, complex multi-tool instruction-following)
OASIS (Reddit-like and Twitter-like social simulation platforms, 100 trajectories per platform)
quantitative_results: ALFWorld-ID average success rate: Baseline 89.5%, RG 78.8% (down 10.7 pts), AgentMark-F 89.3% (down 0.2 pts)
ALFWorld-ID average steps: Baseline 19.7, RG 26.1 (up 6.4), AgentMark-F 19.4 (down 0.3)
ALFWorld-OOD average success rate: Baseline 96.8%, RG 94.5% (down 2.3), AgentMark-F 97.5% (up 0.7)
ALFWorld-OOD average steps: Baseline 15.9, RG 15.4, AgentMark-F 14.1
ToolBench average success rate: Baseline 59.9%, RG 58.5%, AgentMark-F 59.7%
ToolBench average token/step change for AgentMark-F: -6.25% (vs baseline)
Watermark capacity: ALFWorld ~1.19-1.34 bits/step; ToolBench ~0.49 bits/step, 4.93 bits/task

False-positive rate for wrong-key/unwatermarked decoding: below 1% at overhead k>=8, zero observed at k>=14, tracking theoretical 2^-k bound (1000 Monte Carlo trials per k in [0,16], payload length N=128, 281 ToolBench trajectories, 159 unique action indices)

Semantic-preserving rewriting stress test (ALFWorld-OOD, 134 tasks, 2326 steps): Behavior Match Rate 49.45% +/- 16.90%, Average KL divergence 3.227 +/- 1.802, Bit Recovery Rate 16.84% +/- 19.56% (vs. 100%/0.000/100% with no rewriting)

Content-watermark composability (Table 3, ToolBench): AgentMark-F behavioral decoding remains 100% correct when combined with SynthID-Text; SynthID-Text content-detection rate under combined watermarking stays at 96.6%
baselines_compared: Baseline: unwatermarked ReAct-style agent loop directly outputting a planning behavior

RG (Red-Green watermarking, bias-based, from Huang et al. 2025b 'Agent Guide'): keyed-PRG partition of behaviors into a green set (gamma=0.5) with logit bias delta=2.0 added to green behaviors before sampling
SynthID-Text (Dathathri et al. 2024, content/action-layer watermark) used only for the composability experiment, not as a competing behavioral-watermark baseline
